title: Building a Prompt Eval Set
concept: Curating a small labeled set of representative inputs to catch prompt regressions before shipping.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, we learned that 'prompts are code'. What does this mindset' means: what?' is the best practice for managing prompts-as-code mindset. What does this involve?

options.text: Treating prompts as code that requires systematic testing and regression checks
Writing the longest possible prompt to guarantee perfect answers
Constantly changing prompts live in production without testing

exercises.explain: Treating prompts as code means we must test them systematically with reproducible inputs to catch bugs (regressions) before users see them.

cards.title: Fixing One, Breaking Three
cards.body: When you tweak a prompt to fix one mistake, you often accidentally break three other things. How do you make sure your prompt updates actually improve the system overall instead of just shifting the bugs around?

cards.title: What is an Eval Set?
cards.body: An evaluation set (or 'eval set') is a curated collection of representative input examples and their ideal target outputs. It acts as a benchmark to measure how well your prompt performs across different scenarios.

cards.title: The Flight Simulator
cards.body: Think of an eval set like a flight simulator for your prompt. Instead of testing a new plane design by flying it in real weather, you run it through a standard set of simulated storms and crosswinds to see if it still flies straight.

cards.title: An Eval Set in Action
cards.body: For a translation prompt, your eval set might include short idioms, long formal paragraphs, and medical terms. If you change your prompt to sound more polite, you run it against this set to make sure it doesn't break the medical translations.

cards.title: Myth: Evals Require Giant Datasets
cards.body: You don't need thousands of data points to start. In fact, a carefully curated set of just 20 to 50 diverse inputs is often enough to catch the majority of prompt errors and regressions early on.

cards.title: Manual vs. Automated Evals
cards.body: Testing prompts one-by-one by hand is slow and subjective. Running them against a structured eval set gives you immediate, objective feedback.

left.text: Checking single outputs one-by-one in a chat box. Slow and inconsistent.

right.text: Running 30 test cases automatically to see exactly what changed. Fast and reliable.

exercises.prompt: Why is it important to use a fixed, consistent set of inputs when testing prompt updates?

options.text: To make the AI run faster
To provide a consistent benchmark so you can compare prompt versions fairly
To train the LLM's internal weights directly
To replace the need for writing prompts altogether

exercises.explain: An evaluation set acts as a consistent benchmark. If you don't keep the inputs the same, you cannot tell if a change in performance is due to your prompt tweak or the new test inputs.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Your evaluation set should only contain the easiest inputs that the prompt is guaranteed to get right.
exercises.explain: An evaluation set should contain a mix of typical cases, tricky edge cases, and safety checks to ensure the prompt handles all real-world scenarios correctly.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the definition of how eval sets prevent hidden bugs.
exercises.sentence: We run our prompt against a fixed eval set to catch unexpected regressions before deploying.
exercises.explain: An evaluation set helps us run repeatable tests to catch regressions—which are new bugs or drops in quality caused by changes to the prompt.

exercises.prompt: Which of these represents a structured item inside a prompt evaluation set?

options.text: An instruction saying: 'Translate all incoming text accurately and politely.'
A list of the last 5 things a single developer typed during testing today.
Input: 'Apply coupon SAVE20 to my $100 order.' | Target: 'Calculates discount as $20 and updates total.'

exercises.explain: Option C shows a structured pair of a specific test input and the desired target behavior, which defines a high-quality eval set item. Options A and B are just general instructions or simple logs.

exercises.prompt: You are updating a prompt for a customer support bot. Which of the following makes the best evaluation set to test your changes?

options.text: A massive database of 10,000 random customer chat logs
A curated set of 30-50 varied inputs representing common queries, edge cases, and safety checks
A single, perfect input that the prompt always gets right
A list of inputs generated randomly by another AI model without human review

exercises.explain: An evaluation set must represent the real-world variety and difficulty of user inputs, including edge cases, while being small enough to run quickly so you can spot bugs before deploying updates.
